responseSet.title: Thelma & Louise: Trouble in Paradise
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thelma & Louise

Trouble in Paradise

Therese Lichtenstein PHD

Therese Lichtenstein

New York

Therese Lichtenstein   Is an art historian who is a visiting assistant professor at Rice University in Houston, TX. Her articles have appeared in Artforum, Art & Text, and Sulfer. She is also an art critic who lives in New York City.

The film Thelma & Louise (1991) has often been criticized for presenting women with negative role models. This criticism takes the film too literally, failing to recognize how Thelma & Louise, as film fantasy, effects more complex psychological experiences of gender construction. This article explores the psychological fascination of the film as allegorical fantasy.

Announcement

As of March 1993, Psychoanalytic Abstracts replaces a previous publication of the American Psychological Association, PsycSCAN: Psychoanalysis. Like PsycSCAN, Psychoanalytic Abstracts is a quarterly publication that provides abstracts and summaries on psychoanalytic topics from three major sources of information: books, chapters, and serials. Psychoanalytic Abstracts includes indexes in the first three issues each year, and an annual index in the fourth issue of the year. The March issue contains summaries of books and chapters; the September issue contains abstracts on psychoanalytic topics from nonanalytic journals. Psychoanalytic Psychology and other psychoanalytic journals are abstracted and indexed in the publication's June and December issues.

responseSet.title: On Shulman's “The Quantitative Investigation of Psychoanalytic Theory and Therapy: A Bibliography 1986-1992”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Shulman's “The Quantitative Investigation of Psychoanalytic Theory and Therapy

A Bibliography 1986-1992”

Horst Kä MD

Horst Kä

Horst Kä   Is Professor of Psychotherapy, now Chairman of the Department of Psychotherapy, Ulm University, Germany, and Director of the Center for Psychotherapy Research, Stuttgart, Germany. He has trained with the German Psychoanalytic Association and at present acts also as director of the Psychoanalytic Training Institute in Ulm. Since 1970, he has collaborated with Helmut Thomä, establishing the Ulm study group on psychoanalytic process research.

I would like to point out that the 1992 article by Shulman should have modestly been titled “A North American Selection of the Quantitative Investigation of Psychoanalytic Theory and Therapy.” It is to be praised that Shulman tries to raise the research-oriented, quantiative consciousness within the psychoanalytic field. However, some issues have to be solved that might otherwise hamper development.

First, what criteria for psychoanalytic does Shulman use? For example, he includes Klerman's Interpersonal Psychotherapy, which in my view is a far cry from psychoanalysis even when using the very liberal definition of it that we have proclaimed for many years (Thomä & Kächele, 1987, 1992). He also includes quite a few references from the general field of psychotherapy research that are not very psychoanalytic.

Non-North American literature seems to escape the author's collecting efforts; for example, important work by Rainer Krause on emotion and facial action (e.g., Krause & Lütolf, 1988) is totally neglected although it recently has been noticed even by Otto Kernberg in his latest book (Kernberg, 1992). This is even more astonishing as the author seems to have been aware of the collection entitled Psychoanalytic Process Research Strategies (Dahl, Kächele, & Thomä, 1988)

Requests for reprints should be sent to Horst U. Kächele, MD, Department of Psychotherapy, University of Ulm, Am Hochsträss 8, D-89081 Ulm, Germany.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vision and Separation: Between Mother and Baby

Kenneth Wright. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1991, xvii + 370 pp., $40.

Paul Horton MD

Paul Horton

Meriden, CT

Paul Horton   Is a privately practicing psychiatrist in Meriden, CT and a consultant to the Child Guidance Clinic for Central Connecticut.

This book was written by a psychiatrist-psychoanalyst and is intended mainly for classical Freudian psychoanalysts with a philosophical bent. Topics covered include the “preeminent” role of the “mother's face” in development (pp. 9, 109), transitional object attachment, attachment theory in general, the sense of self and other, philosophy, the Bible, consciousness, existentialism, religion, symbol formation, linguistics, introjection, illusion, aspects of the Oedipus complex, metaphor, and the father's role in development. The author's basic thesis is that, somehow, the mother's face becomes the “guiding light of [the baby's] mind” (p. 12)

According to Wright, the mother's face, particularly her smile, is the baby's first transitional object (p. 108), and attachment behavior throughout life can be traced back to this introject. This is explained with reference (p. xv) to only two of Winnicott's articles and without citations of a pertinent and extensive transitional object literature that has developed since the early 1970s. I address the author's scholarship and the many problems with his thesis. First, however, I make some overview comments pertaining to scholarship issues and to tone and style not specific to the transitional object concept.

The author's theory is about the face, the baby's view of the face and its parts, and how this affects emotional development throughout life. To Wright, classical psychoanalysis has nothing to say on the matter: He “noted the almost complete absence of the face in psychoanalytic theory” (p. 333). He asks, “Is there not a gap in psychoanalytic theory, even present-day theory, where the face should be? Are those thick lips and large mouth [referring to the cortical homunculus, p. 1] really all there is to the human face?” (p. 2). In his “search of the psychoanalytic indexes,” it was remarkable how few entries could be found under face; he found a “plethora of references to mouth, breast, penis, vagina” but little or nothing under face (p. 4).

responseSet.title: The Limitation of a Positivistic Approach to Psychoanalytic History: A Reply to Naso's Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Limitation of a Positivistic Approach to Psychoanalytic History

A Reply to Naso's Commentary

Era Loewenstein PHD

Era Loewenstein

California Pacific Medical Center

Era Loewenstein   Is on the Supervisory Faculty of the Department of Psychiatry at California Pacific Medical Center, and on the Teaching Faculty at Mt. Zion Medical Center of the University of California, San Francisco. She is in private practice in San Francisco. Her interests include the psychoanalytic process, the structure of subjectivity, and the narrative dimension of the psychoanalytic process.

A close examination of Naso's (1992) commentary on my article (Loewenstein, 1992) reveals contradictions which point to its author's lack of a clear philosophical commitment. While Naso's statements appear to be informed by a relativistic perspective his argument is constrained by an unarticulated positivistic view-point Naso's criticism of my style, which he describes as metaphoric and evocative, is examined. Contrary to Naso's claim, scientific style does not possess a privileged access to psychoanalytic life history. Moreover, the meanings and affective valences of historical events are not fixed and static, as Naso implies, but contextual and dynamic.

[[RunningHead]]

The Manifest Dream and Its Use in Therapy

Roy M. Mendelsohn. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1990, x + 266 pp., $35.

Martin Stein MD

Martin Stein

New York

Martin Stein   Is a training and supervising analyst at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute. He has written on the “unobjectionable transference,” dreams and states of consciousness, also on irony and on the personality of Samuel Pepys as portrayed in his Diary.

From ancient times, man has attempted to interpret dreams—to foretell the future, to make military and other decisions, to determine the course of illness, and above all to understand the intentions of the gods who sent those dreams. It was the soothsayers who were called on to interpret them, a task that they performed brilliantly on occasion (if we are to credit the accounts that have come down to us). They employed a combination of symbolic understanding, common sense, and principally an intuitive sense of what the dreamer, often a charismatic and violent ruler, wanted to hear. They were not as a rule interested in understanding the unconscious desires of the dreamer, nor did they bother to elicit his or her associations (Stein, 1984). This was left for Freud.

Dr. Mendelsohn is interested in determining the unconscious thoughts and desires of the dreamer and claims that he can discover these by a kind of direct interpretation of the dream text as related by the dreamer, without resorting to the latter's associations. He does so by employing the context in which the dream occurred and his own understanding of symbolism. His method has much in common with the kind of interpretation that Freud, citing Silberer, referred to as anagogic (Freud, 1900, p. 524).

Anagogic interpretation (in this context) may be defined as that which is expressed in abstract and intellectual terms and generally avoids the more fundamental sources of resistance. Such interpretation is likely to be readily accepted by the dreamer, for it arouses little or no anxiety. Freud was dismissive, asserting that it had no place in psychoanalytic work. But he left open whether it could be useful in other types of therapy. Without using the term, many authors (e.g., Kohut, 1977, pp. 108-110) have employed such interpretation, claiming to have found it valuable in understanding clinical progress. Mendelsohn goes much further, however, in his claims for instant understanding and clinical benefit.

responseSet.title: Riposte to Spezzano
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Riposte to Spezzano

Robert Holt PHD

Robert Holt

Robert Holt   Is Professor of Psychology Emeritus, New York University. His most recent book is Freud Reappraised (Guilford, 1989). At his home in Truro, MA, he is putting the finishing editorial touches on his two-volume collection of the papers of Benjamin Rubinstein, after which he will resume work on an intellectual biography of Freud.

The editor has kindly allowed me to respond to Spezzano's spirited defense of Reisner (1991). Empathizing with the latter's uncomfortable position, Dr. Spezzano may well be right in imagining that he feels ambushed. Throughout, this rejoinder to my critique (Holt, 1992) is marked by a clinician's attention to the emotional and interpersonal aspects of the situation: sympathy for a young person just starting a career of scholarly writing; an indulgent tolerance for intellectual errors that are after all very common; and awareness of feelings, which I did not directly avow, of impatience, irritation, and indignation, plus my unfortunate attempt to lighten the tone of condemnation by attempts at wit which sound like pitiless ridicule. He did miss the point of my own final attempt to empathize by confessing that I had, at approximately Reisner's age, made some of the same mistakes, which of course was not intended as a bit of scholarly critique. Likewise, he completely missed my feeling about Reisner's “having dared to quote” me. Far from feeling that he was presumptuous or that only I can legitimately interpret my writings, I nevertheless believe that any author who cites another should do a better job of grasping the latter's meaning. However “creative” it may be, a misunderstanding should be labeled as such by a responsible referee.

What is lacking here is, from my vantage point, the very heart of the matter: concern for objective intellectual standards of scholarly work. To one who, like myself, feels that in the long run our discipline is ill served by tolerance for bad scholarship, however lovingly empathic, it is a regrettable necessity to label it as such.

My first encounter with the article in question was when I read it as a referee for this journal. I found it so filled with errors (several of the worst of which the author quietly corrected) that I advised that it be rejected. At the time, I believe that I did not know the embarrassing fact that the dissertation on which it was based had won a prize.

In The Analysis of the Self, Kohut (1971) asked how one could determine whether a set of theories and the specific form of therapeutic management flowing from them are scientific or inspirational. He answered by proposing three additional questions:

(1) Do we have a systematical theoretical grasp of the processes involved in therapy? (2) Can the treatment method be communicated to others, i.e., can it be learned (and ultimately practiced) without the presence of its originator? And (3), most importantly: Does the treatment method remain successful after the death of its creator? (1971, p. 223n)

More than a decade since Kohut's death, the volume of writing in the field of self psychology continues to expand, testifying to the stimulating challenge of his theories not only to mental health professionals in all disciplines but to those in the humanities as well. The latest contribution, Psychotherapy After Kohut: A Textbook of Self Psychology by Ronald R. Lee and J. Colby Martin, who are psychologists trained in the field of pastoral counseling and steeped in the literature of self psychology, is a welcome arrival.

Their book arose from experience in teaching self psychology to students of psychotherapy at the Illinois School of Professional Psychology. responseSet.title: Significance of the Unconscious Plan for Psychoanalytic Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Significance of the Unconscious Plan for Psychoanalytic Theory

Thomas Rosbrow PHD

Thomas Rosbrow

Thomas Rosbrow   Is a supervising and training analyst and faculty at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, on the faculty of the Professional School of Psychology, San Francisco, and a member of the San Francisco Psychotherapy Research Group. He is in private practice in San Francisco. His interests include unconscious guilt and blame, psychic trauma, reconstruction, and comparative psychoanalytic theory.

Weiss, Sampson, and the San Francisco Psychotherapy Research Group (formerly the Mount Zion Psychotherapy Research Group, 1986) asserted the central role of the patient's unconscious plan in analytic treatment. The analyst listens, albeit unconsciously, to the patient with respect for the patient's capacity to spell out his or her goals, the blocks to achieving these goals, and what the analyst needs to do and avoid doing for these goals to be accomplished. The plan approach is based on a distinct theory of therapy and psychopathology conceived by Weiss and validated through research.

This article first summarizes the key ideas of this approach and then compares and contrasts it with other contemporary theories from the writings of Emde, Schafer, Stolorow, and Bowlby. After highlighting the theory's unique features, I discuss how this point of view offers potential solutions to chronic problems—theoretical, cultural, and clinical—that afflict psychoanalysis today.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Mass of Disputing Doctors”

Some Implications of Theoretical Diversity for Psychoanalytic Practice

Stephen Friedlander PHD

Stephen Friedlander

Knoxville, TN

Stephen Friedlander   Is in private practice in Knoxville, Tennessee. His article in this issue is the first in a series on the relation between theory and practice. A detailed case study of the prohibitive function of theory and the dualism of temporality will be forthcoming, to be followed by a third article using the framework of Lacanian theory to redescribe the entire matter psychoanalytically.

The roots of the relation between psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice are obscured by preconceptions as old as psychoanalysis. Now, the diversity of contemporary theory compels us to revise our view of their relation. This article asserts that an analyst's concurrent participation in the parallel discourses of theory and practice makes clinical work possible. Two other elements in the new perspective are discussed: Theory imposes prohibitions, and clinical process occurs in a framework of dualistic temporality. Despite their apparently esoteric aspect, these concepts belong to a prevailing but hitherto unformulated conception of how theory shapes clinical practice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of the Therapeutic Action in Child Analytic Treatment

Arthur Caspary PHD

Arthur Caspary

Arthur Caspary   Is a private practitioner in Toronto and a core faculty member of the Hincks Institute for Training, Research, and Consultation. He teaches and provides clinical supervision at the Hincks Treatment Centre and is a candidate in the Canadian Psychoanalytic Institute. His interests include the therapeutic action in psychoanalytic treatment as well as the application of psychoanalytic principles to the treatment of high-risk children.

This article attempts to explore certain sources of gain in psychoanalytic work with children. Therapeutic gains are shown to be directly related to the patient's development of the capacity to empathize with his or her own self-representations as experienced and communicated by the therapist. Although the patient's self-representations come to be experienced by the therapist in a variety of ways, the focus in this article is primarily on the patient's use of projective identification. Clinical material is used to illustrate a sequence that involves (a) the projection of initially disowned self-elements, (b) the evocation of corresponding affects and fantasies in the therapist, (c) the patient's empathic scrutiny of the fate of these elements while contained within the therapist, and (d) the eventual reinternalization by the patient of the now therapeutically transformed self- elements. In addition, it is argued that those elements of the therapist's self that are experienced by the patient as related to the projected material will also be reinternalized.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Borderline Personality Organization and Prognosis in Eating Disorders

Claes Norring DMSC

Claes Norring

Claes Norring   Is Research Fellow in the Department of Psychiatry and Director of Research and Development in the Eating Disorders Unit at Uppsala University Hospital, Sweden. His main interests are eating disorders, personality processes and disorders, and treatment outcome research.

A widely held clinical opinion is that borderline functioning in patients with eating disorders implies a long and difficult treatment with an uncertain outcome. Johnson, Tobin, and Dennis (1990) found that borderline bulimics had a markedly worse prognosis over 1 year than bulimics without borderline features. The present study used interview-based assessment of level of personality organization and confirmed the finding in an unselected group of eating disorder patients at 1-year follow-up. However, after 2 and 3 years, no such indications were evident. Instead, the borderline patients unexpectedly caught up, eliminating the initial advantage held by the neurotics, and after 3 years, there were no differences in outcome between the groups. Implications of these results are discussed.

Conflicts Over Selfishness

One Aspect of Some Women's Wish for a Baby

F. Barth MSW CSW

F. Barth

F. Barth   Is in private practice in New York City. She is an instructor and supervisor at the Psychoanalytic Institute of the Postgraduate Center for Mental Health and at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies in New York City. She runs study groups on comparative psychoanalytic theory and on gender identity, and she is on the editorial board of the journal Group. She has written articles on the clinical application of contemporary psychoanalytic theory to classical issues, such as countertransference, obsessional neurosis, and eating disorders. She is currently editing an issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry on the subject of feminine and masculine gender identity and is working on an article on termination in psychoanalysis.

The wish for a baby has traditionally been with femininity in Western culture. However, the connection between the two has never been fully explicated. Traditional psychoanalytic formulations of gender in general and of the wish for a baby in particular have focused on specific genital-based fantasies. In response to this focus, many feminist writers have turned to relational, cultural, and social explanations, but in so doing they have failed to explore other significant intrapsychic factors. This article focuses on one of the potential meanings of the wish for a baby—the organization of a sense of a feminine self around giving to and caring for others, exemplified by the fantasy of the mother caring unambivalently for her baby. This aspect of the wish has both intrapsychic and interpersonal (including social and cultural) impetus and meaning for each individual. Three clinical examples illustrate the significance of this often unconscious and/or unarticulated meaning of the wish for a baby.

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen   Is Clinical Professor of Psychology, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University. Formerly Professor of Anthropology, Lehman college, CUNY, she is the author of several books and many articles on gender; Sexuality; psychoanalytic, feminist, and social theory; and the ethnography of Greece. The provocative style in which Sexual Personae is written tempts one to abandon scholarly etiquette and reply in kind (and, indeed, should this essay occasionally succumb to temptation, I hope the essayist will be forgiven). Yet what inflames some, electrifies others. So, although this book, and its author, Camille Paglia, have outraged most feminists (e.g., Pochoda, 1992; Wolf, 1992), they have excited others (e.g., Bright, 1991; Rich, 1991). Even the mainstream response swings between disdain (e.g., Kakutani's, 1992, review of Paglia's latest book) and admiration (in book-tour interviews, e.g., Rose, 1992). What is this contradiction all about? Just as it is the phenomenon of Camille Paglia, rather than what she has to say, that generates all the excitement, so it is more the sensation than the content of Sexual Personae that is worth reviewing (which was, indeed, her provocative intent, p. 12). This sensationalism does, however, make a certain sense; as we will see, language and style, tone and affect, have a lot to do with what one can and cannot express, may and may not think.

The subject of Sexual Personae is art in all its forms, and its provocative thesis is antifeminist:

Out with stereotypes, feminism proclaims. But stereotypes are the west's stunning sexual personae, the vehicles of art's assault against nature…. From remotest antiquity, western art has been a parade of sexual personae…. Art is form struggling to wake from the nightmare of nature. (p. 39)

A sexual persona is “a projected personality” (p. 76).

Edith Kurzweil

Newark

Edith Kurzweil   Is Professor and Chair of Sociology at Rutgers University, Newark, and Executive Editor of Partisan Review. Among her books are The Age of Structuralism: Lévi-Strauss to Foucault (1980) and The Freudians: A Comparative Perspective (1989). She frequently writes for both professional and intellectual journals.

Both the first and the second volumes of this Histoire de la psychanalyse en France, which appeared in 1982 and 1986, were subtitled La bataille de cent ans (The Hundred Years Battle). But only the second volume of this “battle” was translated into English; it was renamed Jacques Lacan & Co.: A History of Psychoanalysis in France, 1925-1985. In the two volumes, Elisabeth Roudinesco's vivacious and sweeping account spans a century of French intellectual history as the fitting backdrop for Jacques Lacan's ascent to cultural hegemony—from Freud's first visit to Paris in 1885 to 1985. With the benefit of hindsight, she implies that Lacan was destined all along to become the Parisians” intellectual master and hero as well as the heir to Freud's legacy—both to the therapy through dreams of individuals on couches and to the psyches of poets, writers, and political thinkers. Thus Roudinesco makes no bones about the fact that she takes Lacan's side against the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA) with which he broke in 1953 (her mother had been the first student in the SociéAté psychanalytique de Paris [SPP] to confront Lacan's opponents, and remained one of his stalwart supporters), or that she has anything but a parti-pris position.

Although both volumes cover the period between 1925 and 1939, Lacan & Co. alone focuses on the relations among psychoanalysis, surrealism, and the Communist movement. To set up her central argument that Lacan was infinitely more than a doctor with psychoanalytic training, that he had had a literary and political past, Roudinesco begins with his background in Hegelian philosophy and his associations with the Surrealists.

Emotions, Defenses, Coping Mechanisms, and Symptoms

Louis Gottschalk MD PHD , Janny Fronczek MA and Lennart Abel MS

Lennart Abel

Irvine

Lennart Abel   Was a Research Fellow at the Brain Imaging Center, in the Department of Psychiatry and Human Behavior, University of California, Irvine. He is now obtaining a medical degree in Germany.

Empirical research on affects and their defenses has not progressed much beyond the clinical psychoanalytic treatment paradigm initiated by Freud. Recently there has been renewed interest in trying to reach a consensus on the definitions of defenses and in achieving their reliable measurement with subjects within and outside the psychoanalytic situation. One method of measuring defenses that focuses on the major data of observation of psychoanalysis—namely, speech—is the Gottschalk-Gleser (1969) method of content analysis. This article report on a study using this method to examine 5-min samples of speech elicited from six different groups of individuals via purposely ambiguous standardized instructions simulating the request to free-associate. The purpose of this research was to determine how the magnitude of self-acknowledged anxiety and the defenses of anxiety displacement and anxiety denial relate to each other and to a variety of other factors, such as age, sex, intelligence, degree of mental disorder, magnitude of various kinds of hostility, and state of consciousness. The findings indicate that displacements and denials in mentally healthy individuals are more likely to function as coping mechanisms in contrast to their function as defenses or symptoms in mentally disordered people.

Readers who look at the dust jacket of the hardcover version of this book or at the back cover of the paperback will see that I praised it on reading a prepublication version. Your editor, well aware of this, still thinks it appropriate for me to review the book, and I have to agree: Just because I thought highly of it on a first scanning should not disqualify me from more through reconsideration, which has resulted in even more enthusiastic approval.

The subtitle accurately specifies the scope of the volume, and the title, Personology, identifies the book with the tradition of Henry A. Murray and the Harvard Psychological Clinic (Murray et al., 1938; White, 1966), which focused on idiographic, holistic, psychodynamic personality assessment. Its dedication to Silvan Tomkins, recently deceased, matches Tomkins's (1991) dedication of volume 3 of his Affect, Imagery, Consciousness to Irving Alexander: The Murray psychodynamic tradition is mediated through Alexander's early apprenticeship, career-long friendship, and intellectual exchange with Tomkins. Tomkins's script theory is visible, whereas the Murray scaffolding of needs and press is not, and the logic of Alexander's personological inferences, as he says, is much the same as Tomkin's application (1947) of Mill's canons of inference to Thematic Apperception Test protocols. But Alexander has internalized Tomkins's conceptual resources so securely that he employs them inobtrusively.

The book is relevant to modern psychoanalysis in two distinct ways.

Perhaps unconscious pressures guided Freud in the further development of his thought beyond the seduction theory; however, significant conscious reasons led him to the insufficiency of that theory as a complete explanation for hysteria. Freud's evidence had not come from spontaneous expressions by patients of their childhood sexual abuse but from his pressing his theory on them. Furthermore, he then found himself no better able to explain his patients' behavior than his colleagues with other theories. Most particularly, all these patients left his care, and he was confronted with a collapse of his practice. He nonetheless continued both clinically and theoretically to take account of patients' spontaneous reports of childhood sexual abuse. Freud's negative reaction to Ferenczi on this subject was strongly influenced by Freud's knowledge of Ferenczi's personal state.

Cupid's Misses

Relational Vicissitudes in the Analyses of Single Women

Naomi Rucker PHD

Naomi Rucker

Los Angeles

Naomi Rucker   Is a practicing psychoanalyst in San Diego, CA. She is a member of the Faculty and a Supervising and Training Analyst at the Institute of Contemporary psychoanalysis in Los Angeles and a member of the San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. She has published a number of articles on varied areas of Psychoanalytic theory and process, including symbiotic relatedness, wisdom and intimacy, and dreams and unconscious communication.

This article addresses the interface between adult needs for intimacy and the psychoanalytic process between a certain population of single female patients and their male analysts. Relational vicissitudes of this patient-analyst pairing are discussed from a perspective that is both psychoanalytic and psychosocial. Influences of gender differences, sociological factors, and the existential nature of prolonged unchosen singlehood on the female analysand's subjective experience comprise the early sections of the article. This discussion is followed by an exploration of the ramifications of singlehood for the analytic process and for the analytic relationship. The concepts of primary intimacy and a primary relational void are introduced, and clinical illustrations are incorporated.

[[RunningHead]]

Reading the Transference in Adolescent Psychotherapy

A Comparison of Novice and Experienced Therapists

Elizabeth Church PHD

Elizabeth Church

St. John's Canada

Elizabeth Church   Is an Assistant Professor at the Counselling Center of Memorial University in St. John', Newfoundland. Her research interests include the nature of expertise in psychotherapy and the therapeutic relationship. She is currently working on a feminist-psychodynamic study of the experience of stepmothers.

This study compared how novice and experienced therapists deal with the therapeutic relationship. Hoffman and Gill's (1988a, 1988b) “patient's experience of the relationship with the therapist” method was used to identify adolescent patients' allusions to the transference. The experienced therapists were quite active in responding to the transference allusions, whereas the novices rarely commented on them. The experienced therapists made the transference a central focus of therapy, and almost all of their transference interventions were accepted by the adolescents. By contrast, the novices appeared to feel quite threatened by their patients' overt and covert transference feelings, and their patients frequently rejected their therapy-related interventions. Based on the results of the study, some suggestions for training are made.

This book, which consists of original articles and is dedicated to Saul Scheidlinger and edited by Saul Tuttman, will be of great interest to the practicing analytic group therapist. It can also provide the individual analyst with an excellent overview of the current thinking of some of the leading figures in psychodynamic group therapy.

Saul Scheidlinger has been a prominent force in group therapy in the United States for the past 40 years. He has contributed significantly to the professional education of generations of group therapists in his capacities as editor of the International Journal of Group Psychotherapy and as a professor in the Department of Psychiatry at the Albert Einstein College of Medicine.

Each of the authors has written about topics of group theory and group therapy that have absorbed Scheidlinger's interest over the years. These include explication of key psychoanalytic concepts (especially identification, regression, and the group as a “mother”) and application of them to group therapy. He has also written extensively on empathy and interpretation, the dynamics of leadership, sex education, research in group therapy, scapegoating phenomena, the treatment of a wide variety of socially disadvantaged patients, and group approaches to the treatment of children and adolescents.

The list of authors who have written articles for this volume reads like a Who's Who of American and Canadian group therapy. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Special Issue on Women, Psychoanalysis, and Gender

Editors' Introduction

Adria Schwartz PHD and Donna Bassin PHD

Donna Bassin

Co-Editors

Donna Bassin   Is a private practitioner in New York City, She was trained at the Institute of Psychoanalytic Training and Research and is currently the Director of their Women's Resource Center as well as one of the founding members of their new section on gender, she is on the faculty at the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Training Center and at the Union Institute, She has published in the area of women, gender, and psychoanalysis, and her latest endeavors include a coedited volume, Representations of Motherhood (Yale University Press, 1993).

This special issue on Women, Psychoanalysis, and Gender is a commemoration of the capability, influence, acceptance, and authority of women in the community of psychoanalysis. Although the idea for the volume germinated through the efforts of Adria Schwartz, under the past presidential tenure of Judie Alpert and the Section III Board, and flowered with the generous support and assistance of Bert Cohler, Editor of Psychoanalytic Psychology, its origin must be located in the earlier vision of Barbara Claster, whose interest in women's issues in mental health stimulated the formation of an ad hoc committee on women in psychoanalysis, which would officially become Section III. This was followed by a series of columns written by Bernice Barber, Ruth Formanek, and Dale Mendell for the Division 39 newsletter.

In this issue, we turn to both new and more established voices in an expanded arena of gender, culture, and psychoanalysis. Our authors are critical, generative, playful, and disruptive of the traditional ways in which psychoanalysis has constructed gender. We look at gender in theory, in the consulting room, in popular culture, and on the dramatic and cinematic stage. This particular selection of articles and reviews attempts to represent the breadth of interests in our community within the context of current thinking about gender in psychoanalytic theory and practice. It is but a sampling of the many articles submitted, reflecting the need for a stronger voice for women and gender theorists within the mainstream of psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

The Holt .45 Misfires

Yet Another Round of Lament and Ridicule Aimed at Psychoanalytic Theorizing

Charles Spezzano PHD

Charles Spezzano

Charles Spezzano   Is a psychoanalyst in private practice in San Francisco, and a faculty member and supervising analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. He is the author of a book of essays, What to Do Between Birth and Death (Morrow, 1992), and of Affect in Psychoanalytic Theory and Practice: Toward a New Synthesis (The Analytic Press, in press), an effort to extract a clinically useful theory of affects from the psychoanalytic literature.

Robert Holt (1992) takes Steven Reisner (1991) to task for offering a reading of Freud that highlights an object-relational thread running through Freud's writings. According to Holt, such rereadings of Freud are a waste of time; the real problem to which analytic scholars need to address themselves is the sorry epistemic state of psychoanalytic theory. This commentary counterargues that Holt is simply one of a small, but highly visible, group of lamenters for the alleged terminal epistemic illness of psychoanalysis—a group whose modus operandi has been to ridicule anyone who sees in the traditional forms of psychoanalytic theorizing the potential to say anything useful about human psychology. Reisner has, in fact, made an interesting, exciting, and useful contribution to psychoanalytic thinking and to the wider effort of human beings to capture and accurately represent their unconscious processes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter From the Section President

Randy Milden PHD

Randy Milden

President, Section III, 1992-1993

Randy Milden  

Dear Division 39 members,

On behalf of Section III, Women and Psychoanalysis, I am delighted to introduce our special issue of Psychoanalytic Psychology. Section III has had a vital history within the Division. Over the last several years, we have worked in a number of arenas to join women's issues and feminist concerns with the project of psychoanalytic psychology.

Psychologists associated with the Section have been at the forefront of contemporary psychoanalytic writing on women and gender, addressing issues related to female development: sexuality, intimacy, and work in women's lives; and gender in psychoanalytic theory and practice. This effort at greater inclusiveness in psychoanalytic thought has not been simply one of filling a gap or of redressing the exclusion of women by “adding and stirring.” Rather, as we have revised and refined psychoanalytic perspectives on gender, we have participated in a transformation of psychoanalytic thought, more broadly framed.

The articles and reviews in this issue, thoughtfully compiled by the Guest Editors, Adria Schwartz, PhD, and Donna Bassin, PhD, reflect this transformation. We are proud to have the opportunity to present this collection, originally conceived under the tenure of Judie Alpert, PhD, and nurtured by Carole Morgan, PhD, both past presidents of Section III, and we are grateful to the Board of Division 39 and to the Editor of Psychoanalytic Psychology, Bertram Cohler, PhD, for their commitment to our task.

[[RunningHead]]

Fantasy

A Consolidation

Doris Silverman PHD and Ruth Gruenthal MSS

Ruth Gruenthal

Ruth Gruenthal   Is faculty and supervisor, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University; and training analyst, faculty, and senior supervisor, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health. Her teaching and writing have centered on self psychology and she is a member of The National Council for Psychoanalytic Self Psychology.

Via the role of fantasy, we propose a theoretical integration. Specifically, we focus on the pathologically conflicted aspects of sensual-sexual and aggressive strivings as well as the self-organizing and reparative aspects that contribute to fantasy formation. We present key aspects of Kohut's thinking to set the stage for a self-psychological view of fantasy. We also discuss contemporary self-psychological positions on fantasy. We suggest the limitations of this single motivational focus and then provide a rationale and research data supporting the inclusion of sensual-sexual and aggressive strivings as contributors to fantasy formation and maintenance. Anecdotal and clinical vignettes are offered to suggest different pathways that specific motives take to initiate and sustain fantasy life; we demonstrate, as well, the intertwining of motives. We maintain that such a theoretical consolidation lends efficacy to clinical work.

[[RunningHead]]

Dreaming is Play

Kelly Bulkeley PHD

Kelly Bulkeley

Kelly Bulkeley   Is a Visiting Scholar at the Graduate Theological Union at the University of California, Berkeley. He is the author of The Wilderness of Dreams (State University of New York Press, in press) and of numerous articles on the relation of dreams to psychology, religion, philosophy, politics, culture, and child development. He is currently editing a volume on dreams and social change, Dreams (State University of New York Press), and is writing a book on the role of dreams in the world's religious and spiritual traditions.

The psychoanalytic understanding of dreams has traditionally been expressed in metaphorical terms drawn from the physical sciences; since Freud, we have conceptualized dreams as a kind of work. The “dreaming is work” metaphor, although originally helpful, has come to impede the further development of psychoanalytic knowledge about dreams. This article argues that a “dreaming is play” metaphor provides us with a more helpful conceptual framework for understanding dreams. By seeing dreams as a kind of play, we are able to (a) make better sense of new research on the formation and the functions of dreams and (b) appreciate certain valuable new roles for dreams in contemporary Western culture.

Postpartum Depression and Symbiotic Illusion

Hendrika Halberstadt-Freud PHD

Hendrika Halberstadt-Freud

Amsterdam

Hendrika Halberstadt-Freud   Is a psychoanalyst of children and adults and works as a private practitioner, training analyst, and supervisor in Amsterdam. She has written on psychodiagnostics with children and adolescents, perversion, and female development, specifically regarding the mother—daughter relationship. Her latest book is Freud, Proust, Perversion and Love (Swets & Zeitlinger, 1991).

A review of the literature reveals how little has been published by psychoanalysts about the important subject of postpartum depression. This article offers a way of looking at the phenomenon from a transgenerational perspective. The concept of symbiosis is taken from the mother's point of view as well as from the baby's. It is defined as a delusional two-person fantasy of mutual dependence rather than as a normal phase of development. The analysis of a case of postnatal depression is presented to illustrate the clinical and theoretical implications of this viewpoint and how it was applied in treatment.

responseSet.title: Freud's Rationale for Abandoning the Seduction Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Rationale for Abandoning the Seduction Theory

Joel Kupfersmid PHD

Joel Kupfersmid

Twinsburg, OH

Joel Kupfersmid   Worked as a psychologist for more than 15 years in a children's psychiatric hospital. He is currently a psychologist in a day-treatment program for emotionally disturbed children and adolescents. He recently co-authored An Author's Guide to Publishing Better Articles in Better Journals in the Behavioral Sciences.

Currently, there are several positions on why Freud abandoned the seduction theory. As a means to better understand Freud's rationale, this article analyzes the reasons Freud gave in print and in private letters. The conclusion drawn is that there is only minor support for the traditional view that his belief in the Oedipus complex led Freud to abandon the seduction theory. This analysis suggests that many of the reasons Freud gave for rejecting seduction theory, both in public and in private, were not strongly held convictions.

[[RunningHead]]

Gender Identities, Lesbianism, and Potential Space

Beverly Burch PHD

Beverly Burch

Oakland, CA

Beverly Burch   Is in private practice in Oakland, CA and teaches at the New College of California in San Francisco. Her book, On Intimate Terms: The Psychology of Difference in Lesbian Relationships (University of Illinois Press, 1992), was recently published. She is particularly interested in the continuing reappraisal of women's developmental issues.

This article offers a conception of individual gender identity as multifaceted rather than unitary, arguing that traditional conceptions of gender force a false dichotomy. Lesbian relationships afford a degree of freedom from the conventional restrictions of gender and need to be explored within this context. Traditional psychoanalytic theory posits masculine identity as the basis for lesbian development. I argue that lesbian identity instead reflects a diversity of representations and identifications that may be drawn on in intimate connections. This fluidity can be understood in the terms of Winnicott's (1971) conception of potential space with its possibilities for creativity and psychic expansiveness. The reparative power of psychic play in lesbian relationships can lead to an enlarged sense of what is feminine.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition and Incomplete Mourning

The Intergenerational Transmission of Traumatic Themes

Peter Shabad PHD

Peter Shabad

Peter Shabad   Is an Assistant Professor of Clinical Psychiatry at Northwestern University Medical School. He is also in private practice in Chicago. He is co-editor of The Problem of Loss and Mourning: Psychoanalytic Perspectives (International Universities Press, 1989) and is currently working on a book integrating psychoanalytic theory with existential thought.

This article addresses the tension in character development between the compulsion to repeat and the urge toward growth, between adhering to the old and pushing toward the new. Specifically, it is suggested that the successful interchange of giving and receiving with the previous generation enables the child to move onward with good conscience. When this interchange breaks down, it may become fixed around a traumatic theme. Identification with the parental aggressor of the traumatic theme may then become the source for a characterological immersion in the compulsion to repeat. The intergenerational transmission of one's traumatic themes to one's children is viewed as a manifestation of the identification with the aggressor and is contrasted with the process of mourning one's one and only childhood. Mourning is viewed as a process of reintegrating repressed wishes for “what could have been.” This reintegration, in turn, enables one to relinquish the necessity that those wishes be fulfilled, and makes it possible to give a better life to one's children.

responseSet.title: The Mystery of Homosexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mystery of Homosexuality

Ken Corbett PHD

Ken Corbett

New York

Ken Corbett   Is in private practice in New York City. His current interests include the psychoanalytic theories of male homosexuality and masculinity He is also interested in distinguishing between homosexual boyhood and gender identity disorder.

Psychoanalysts have repeatedly attempted to (dis)locate homosexuals within a theory of gender that rests on essential distinctions between what is feminine and what is masculine. The best illustration of this (dis)location is the manner in which the male homosexual has been regarded as feminine. Calling gay men feminine neither sufficiently problematizes their experience of gender nor adequately captures the vicissitudes of gender. I contend that male homosexuality is a differently structured masculinity, not a simulated femininity. Though both heterosexual and homosexual men readily identify themselves as men, the gay man's gender identity is distinguished by his experience of passivity in relation to another man. I maintain that a crucial step in the treatment of any gay man is the recognition of his early gender experience and of how that experience is knit into the fabric of his sexuality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shadow and Substance

A Relational Perspective on Clinical Process

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

New York

Philip Bromberg   is a Fellow and a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Psychoanalytic Institute and a Clinical Professor of Psychology at the New York University Postdoctoral Program. His major interest focuses on the relational context of the psychoanalytic process and the treatment of character pathology.

In this article, I suggest that one aspect of human personality structure is usefully understood as a dynamic balance or dialectic between dissociation and conflict and argue that psychoanalysis must continue to broaden its concepts of psychic structure, unconscious phenomena, and therapeutic action beyond the model provided by conflict theory. From a relational perspective, an analyst is not simply helping a patient change a unified though poorly adaptive self-representation to an equally unified more adaptive one, but is also negotiating with multiple self-representations, each in its own terms, and enabling the dissociative gaps among them to rebuild linkages through a perceptual process of meaning construction. The roles of enactment and interpretation are discussed in the context of trauma, dissociation, and the nature of reality.

Paul Trad   Is Director of the Child and Adolescent Outpatient Department of the New York Hospital—Cornell Medical Center, Westchester Division. He is also Assistant Professor of Psychiatry at Cornell University Medical College.

Erna Furman's insightful new work, Toddlers and Their Mothers, describes her affirmative effort to place the toddler in the forefront of diagnostic and treatment protocols. She focuses on a population often overlooked by child health care workers. Straddling the tenuous domain between infancy and the early school years, toddlerhood remains the forgotten era. Furman skillfully articulates the noteworthy maturational challenges that confront children of this age and, in so doing, awakens the reader to the remarkable potential that characterizes children during these years of pivotal transformation.

The discussion presented in the book is both compelling and personable. One reason why the narrative is so compelling is that Furman has relied on a model of intervention with which she has had extensive experience and can therefore discuss her insights in a refreshingly unpretentious and conversational style. The Mother—Toddler Group, of the Hanna Perkins School adopts a dramatically different approach to the assessment and treatment of the toddler. The Mother—Toddler Group, which derives its name from the inclusion of both mother and toddler in a joint effort with other dyads, has devised a series of interventions to address several of the prime developmental tasks that children of this age must master to function effectively in the world. Specifically, Furman devotes substantial attention to the self-care of bodily needs and to the gradual relinquishment of control by the parent as the toddler masters an independent mode of functioning. Highlighting the key role of the caregiver, especially of the mother, in helping the child organize these skills, Furman advocates treatment-via-the-parent

Female Homosexuality: Choice Without Volition—A Psychoanalytic Study

Elaine V. Siegel. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1988, xxiii + 239 pp., $32.50.

Margaret Buttenheim PHD and Susan Contratto EDD

Susan Contratto

Ann Arbor

Susan Contratto   Is previous Director of the Women's Studies Program at the University of Michigan and has taught at the University in the Departments of Psychiatry, Psychology, and Women's Studies. She has published articles on mothering from psychodynamic, cultural, and historical perspectives. She has also written on the generational transmission of perspectives on power within families. Her recent work is on the goodness of fit between Gilligan's developmental theory and clinical practice, and she has written on cure from the perspective of a feminist practitioner. She is in private practice in Ann Arbor, MI.

In Female Homosexuality: Choice Without Volition, Elaine Siegel makes her theoretical lineage and clinical goals explicit from the outset; she dedicates the book to Charles Socarides, MD, and credits her “immersal in and application of” his work with the fact that “some of [her] patients were able to become heterosexual” (p. xiv). Socarides is best known for his theories about developmental deficits in male homosexuals and for his ardent work in the 1970s to persuade the American Psychiatric Association to maintain homosexuality as a psychiatric diagnosis. Siegel applies his schematization of male homosexuality to female homosexuals, with a twist. She argues that whereas male homosexuals tend to overvalue their genitals, female homosexuals have failed to “take full possession of their vaginas” (p. 22), a failure that both she (p. 22) and Theodore L. Dorpat (p. xv) in his introduction refer to as a “calamity.” The bulk of the book consists of elaborate case presentations of eight of Siegel's analysands, preceded and followed by several theoretical chapters, one of which describes Siegel's “psychoanalytic armamentarium” (p. 32).

Like Socardies, Siegel regards homosexuality as evidence of a developmental arrest. The homosexual woman, she maintains, has failed to incorporate her vagina into her self-representation. Her sexual relationships with women in adulthood reflect an attempt to consolidate her female body image by identifying with the partner's genitals. Implicit in this explanation is the idea that a sexual relationship between two women is necessarily narcissistic. That is, a woman is not enjoyed, cherished, or chosen for her intrinsic attributes; rather, her body is made use of to correct a psychological defect in her partner.

[[RunningHead]]

The Double Life of Véronique

An Inquiry Into the Existence of Woman

Ellie Ragland PHD and Elizabeth Wright DPhil

Elizabeth Wright

Cambridge

Elizabeth Wright   Is Fellow and Lecturer in German at Girton College, Cambridge, England. Among her publications are Psychoanalytic Criticism: Theory in Practice (Routledge, 1984) and Postmoderm Brecht: A Re-Presentation (Routledge, 1989), and she has edited Feminism and Psychoanalysis: A Critical Dictionary (Blackwell, 1992). She is currently writing on feminist crime fiction

This article is an analysis of the film La Double Vie de Véronique; (The Double Life of Véronique; de la Fuente & Kieslowski, 1991) from a Lacanian psychoanalytic perspective. It explores significant differences in the experiences of two women, roles played by the same actress, presented in the film as identical in appearance, living in different places at the same time, and sharing similar life challenges and opportunities. The differences manifest themselves in their relation to the women's remarkable singing ability, their lovers, and familial figures. The early loss of the primary object, the mother, determines both women's being—one of the women more on the side of the normative woman, covering over the loss at the price of death, the other mourning the loss more subtly but no more immune to the forces within her. Both demonstrate Lacan's (1975) axiom that “Woman does not exist,” namely, that she lives precariously near the real, on the boundaries of the symbolic and the imaginary.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Erotic Transference Through Countertransference

The Female Therapist and the Male Patient

Helene Russ PHD

Helene Russ

Seattle

Helene Russ   Is a private practitioner who also teaches in the graduate counseling program of Antioch University, Seattle. She is associated with the Northwest Alliance for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.

Although sexual drama infuses therapy, and the sexual transference and countertransference reactions between female clinician and male patient are rich with potential, few theorists address the reactions publicly for myriad reasons. Parallel to the dearth of literature is the lingering belief that male patients develop only muted sexual transferences, which they have difficulty articulating. This article explores the prevailing theories and focuses on the female clinician's countertransference as the primary limiting factor in sexual transference. The female therapist, who may be unconsciously fearful of losing a boundary or being violated, may afford her male patient a less than optimal chance of understanding the shame attendant to his sexuality or the aggression that may be associated with it. Although sexual transference is the entree into the patient's psychic life, sexual countertransference is anathema, a double standard that is now coming under fire. Two cases are described in which men develop strong sexual transferences and engender strong countertransferences in return. Although these men were sexually overstimulated as children and possibly abused, they may teach us, from the processes they undergo, to extrapolate from their experience to other male patient-female clinician dyads.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Projective Identification and Bulimia

Diane Rozen PHD

Diane Rozen

Diane Rozen   Is in private practice in Beverly Hills and a graduate psychoanalyst on the faculty at the California Graduate Institute in Westwood. She is also an affiliate member at the Psychoanalytic Center of California in Los Angeles. Her interests and teaching focus on developmental theory (particularly prenatal and early development) and the psychoanalytic treatment of bulimia.

This article utilizes the work of Melanie Klein to examine the role of projective identification in the bulimic symptom of binging and self-induced vomiting. It is hypothesized that a severe disturbance in early development, when splitting and projective identification dominate, contributes to the evolution of this symptom. Such an early disturbance skews all later development and is manifested in a variety of conditions, one of which is bulimia. To examine this symptom, Klein's metaphor of the infant and the breast is used. Clinical material illustrates a constellation of phantasies that underlie general and specific patterns of beliefs and behaviors in relation to food and to internal and external objects.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on the Constructions of Maternal Representations

Adria Schwartz PHD

Adria Schwartz

New York

Adria Schwartz   A psychoanalyst in independent practice in New York City, has a longstanding interest in a feminist-informed psychoanalysis, She has written on issues of gender, sexuality, and, most recently, maternal representation. Currently on the faculties of the Department of Psychiatry at Mt. Sinai Medical Center, Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy, and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Study Center, she is President of Section III, Division 39.

Given a deconstructive shift in the conception of mothers and mothering from a unitary category of women in a biological relationship with their offspring to a more fluid constellation of active maternal relations, transcending gender, and constructed through the work of intersubjective dyads, this article asks how we might begin to think about the representation and internalization of maternal relations. With reference to Bowlby's (1980) concept of internal working models of attachment, and to more recent infant research, the concept of maternal functions (specifically, functions of security, regulation, and recognition) is introduced as central to the mothering relation.

[[RunningHead]]

Nostalgic Objects of our Affection

Mouring, Memory, and Maternal Subjectivity

Donna Bassin PHD

Donna Bassin

Donna Bassin   Is a private practitioner in New York City, She was trained at the Institute of Psychoanalytic Training and Research and is currently the Director of their Women's Resource Center as well as one of the founding members of their new section on gender, she is on the faculty at the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Training Center and at the Union Institute, She has published in the area of women, gender, and psychoanalysis, and her latest endeavors include a coedited volume, Representations of Motherhood (Yale University Press, 1993).

In this article, I discuss a nostalgic use of history that reflects arrested mourning of the early transformational mother who “makes things happen.” Exemplified in aspects of popular culture, the past is recalled in foraging and collecting of “vintage retro” items. Resistance to mourning ties the flea-market junkie to an endless elegiac search for “keepsakes from the past” that will “complete the collection” and provide the self with the fantasy of transformational experience and capacity. In the analytic transference, it ties the patient to a bittersweet process of reenacting and becoming. The legacy of transformational capacities from the early mother are tied and wistfully sought in an array of historical objects of affection and desire. This nostalgic relation to memory prevents a true identification with the transformational aspects of maternal practice. In contrast, “mature memorial activity” (Loewald, 1980) represents the acceptance of loss and the potential for an animated and dynamic use of memory in the creation of a generative maternal self.

On Feminism and Psychoanalysis—In the Case of Anorexia Nervosa

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl PHD

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl

Haverford College

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl   Is Professor at Haverford College, Haverford, PA. Among other works, she is the author of two biographies, Hannah Arendt (Yale University Press, 1982) and Anna Freud (Summit, 1988), and a recently published monograph, Creative Characters (Routledge, 1991).

This article focuses on the 1970s therapeutic literature on anorexia nervosa that tried to establish the claim that “fat is a feminist issue” (Orbach, 1978). This focus is meant to provide a way to consider how feminist social criticism and psychoanalytic theory did and did not meet at that time and over that topic. Anorexia nervosa, it is argued, is a paradigmatic case, revealing the theoretical issues that need to be engaged as the vexed dialogue between feminism and psychoanalysis continues.

[[RunningHead]]

Pathological Narcissism in an Eight-Year-Old Boy

An Example of Bellak's TAT and CAT Diagnostic System

David Abrams PHD

David Abrams

NY

David Abrams   Is chief Psychologist, Center for Preventive Psychiatry, White Plains, NY; Clinical Associate Professor in Clinical Psychology, City University of New York; and faculty and supervisor at the Institute of Child, Adolescent, and Family Studies (ICAFS) in New York City. He is refining a method of analyzing defense/affect intrapsychic conflict on thematic tests that is also applicable to treatment and is writing a book on Freud's case of Little Hans.

Narcissistic pathology is difficult to assess in psychological testing of children and adults. Contemporary psychoanalytic diagnosis is based on Freud's (1915) metapsychological points of view and A. Freud's (1965) developmental profile, as in Schafer's (1954) Rorschach approach and Bellak's (1986) system for the Thematic Apperception Test and Children's Apperception Test. Bellak (1986) provided an analysis of the relation of the ego (self-concept characteristics of the main hero, conception of the world, interpersonal object relations, defense mechanisms, and 12 ego functions), id (thematic analysis, needs, anxieties, and significant conflicts), and superego. A case example of narcissistic pathology in a young child's Children's Apperception Test demonstrates this method of psychoanalytic diagnosis.

[[RunningHead]]

Men Masquerading as Women

Imposture, Illusion, and Dénouement in the Play, M. Butterfly

Helen Gediman PHD

Helen Gediman

Helen Gediman   Is Clinical Professor of Psychology in the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is also a training and supervising analyst and faculty member of the New York Freudian Society and a member of the International Psychoanalytical Association. In private in New York City, she has also published numerous articles in psychoanalytic journals and has recently written a book entitled Fantasies of Love and Death.

In the play, M. Butterfly (Hwang, 1989), the experience of being duped by a man masquerading as a woman is shared by protagonist and audience alike. In a parallel process, both simultaneously know and do not know the realities of the impostor's gender. Cross-dressing, as a variety of imposture, is explained by expanding on Freud's (1927) contribution of the “fantasy of the phallic woman.” A full psychoanalytic understanding of perverse and fetishistic resolutions of early developmental conflicts about the nature of gender requires a consideration of the fear of the vagina along with the wish for a woman with a phallus. This approach to a study of imposture suggests that fantasies shared in common by all people help to account for the success of even gross impostures.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Primitive Defenses and the Borderline Patient's Perceptions of the Psychiatric Treatment Team

Les Greene PHD

Les Greene

Veterans Affairs Medical Center
West Haven, CT

Les Greene   Serves on the staff of the West Haven VA Medical Center and supervises psychotherapy in the Departments of Psychology and Psychiatry of Yale University. His theoretical and research interests are in the areas of psychoanalytic group process and character pathology.

This study investigated the construct validity of several recently devised self-report measures of splitting and related primitive defense mechanisms and undertook a preliminary quantitative analysis of the relation of these defenses to here-and-now social perceptions in borderline psychopathology. Based on an integrated framework of object-relations theory, psychoanalytic group psychology, and social cognition theory, the study explored associations between the use of primitive defenses by borderline inpatients and their perceptions of the various staff roles that constitute their treatment teams (e.g., individual psychotherapist and ward chief).

Multivariate statistical analyses revealed that the reliance on image-distorting defenses and the holding of fragmented images of self as omnipotent and as “bad” were significantly associated with borderlines’ perceived proximity to specific members and subgroups of their treatment teams. The findings further validate selected measures of primitive defenses and suggest that such defenses serve a variety of self needs.

[[RunningHead]]

Response to Flaks's “Homophobia and the Psychologist's Role in Psychoanalytic Training Institutes”

Ralph Roughton MD

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton   Is a training and supervising psychoanalyst and the former Director of the Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute in Atlanta. A Fellow of the Board on Professional Standards and a member of several committees and task forces of the American Psychoanalytic Association, he has been appointed to chair its new Committee on Issues of Homosexuality.

I would like to respond to David Flaks's (1992) article, “Homophobia and the Psychologist's Role in Psychoanalytic Training Institutes,” by informing readers of recent steps taken by the American Psychoanalytic Association (the American).

In April 1992, by an overwhelming majority vote in the Executive Council, the words “training and supervising analysts” were added to our nondiscrimination policy statement to remove any doubt that training and supervising analysts are included in the phrase “all grades of faculty” of our previous policy statement.

Furthermore, the current leadership of the American—Bernard Pacella (President) and Marvin Margolis (Chairman, Board on Professional Standards)—has appointed a Joint Committee on Issues of Homosexuality, whose purpose is to create awareness of problems that may persist with regard to bias and discrimination and to facilitate appropriate changes in attitude and policy. The committee will hold workshops for institute representatives; advise on curriculum; and offer individual consultations to institute leaders, faculty members, and candidates who have problems to discuss.

As the Chair of this new committee, I want to assure the readers that the issue of discrimination and sexual orientation is being taken very seriously by the American. I will gladly discuss this with anyone who would like to contact me personally (1175 Peachtree Street, Atlanta, GA 30361; 404-892-7561).

Homophobia and Psychoanalysis

A Reply to Roughton

David Flaks JD, PsyD

David Flaks

Bala Cynwyd, PA

David Flaks   Is an attorney and a psychology resident with the Lambda Resources Network of BFC Associates, a multicultural, multidisciplinary psychotherapy practice in Bala Cynwyd, PA. His interest include psychoanalytic psychotherapy with lesbian and gay clients, and he has written and presented in the area of lesbian parenting.

Ralph Roughton's response to my article, “Homophobia and the Psychologist's Role in Psychoanalytic Training Institutes” (Flaks, 1992), outlines recent developments within the American Psychoanalytic Association. This commentary commends the attempts to combat homophobia within the organization and suggests potential problems that might limit their success.

[[RunningHead]]

Female Perversions: The Temptations of Emma Bovary

Louise J. Kaplan. New York: Doubleday, 1991, 580 pp., $24.95.

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

New York

Adrienne Harris   Is an Associate Professor of Psychology at Rutgers University and on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is the coeditor (with Lewis Aron) of The Legacy of Sandor Ferenczi (The Analytic Press, in press) and has written on problems of gender and gender development. Her other interests include language, the development of symbolic and presymbolic experience, and issues in psychoanalytic theory. She is in private practice in New York City.

Female Perversions examines the subtle and surprising presence of perversion hidden within gender stereotypes and practices, both banal and dramatic. In this book, Louise Kaplan launches an ambitious project to reframe the analysis of perversion (more typically drawn on male experience) to encompass women's experiences, strategies, and inner lives. Even in normative readings of psychopathology, she finds a gender asymmetry. There is an elaborated discourse in psychoanalytic theory on male perversion and comparatively an absence in the reading of female perversion. Kaplan fills this gap in the psycho-analytic story with a disruptive and trenchant set of ideas.

It is Kaplan's highly original idea that female perversion expresses itself as pathologies of gender rather than pathologies of sexuality. It is an approach that depends on psychoanalytic modes of thought turned on theory, cultural formations, and ideology as well as on psychic life. She finds the disowned wish behind reaction formation and idealization and, in deconstructing normative gender ideology, locates its underlying nasty little secret: Perversion and corruption are often disguised as conformity. She takes this point a radical step further to note that family structures and family dynamics (lived out consciously and unconsciously) contain and maintain gender conformity and thus provoke, produce, and reproduce perversion.

Turning to the wider cultural setting in which these relations are embedded and maintained, Kaplan proposes that we consider ways that perversion is coopted in the efforts to promote commercialism and consumption. Female desire transformed into perversion is a kind of deep internal market for goods and services presented as unremarkable aspects of bourgeois capitalism and normative feminine life style and habits of consumption.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Adrienne Harris, PhD, 5th Floor, 80 University Place, New York, NY 10003.

[[RunningHead]]

Two Methods of Assessing Unconscious Communication in Psychotherapy

Robert Langs MD , Wilma Bucci PHD , Andrea Udoff MA , Gail Cramer PHD and Lenore Thomson MDiv

Lenore Thomson

Orangeburg, NY

New York

New York

Lenore Thomson   is affiliated with the C. G. Jung Foundation for Analytic Psychology. Her primary research interest is in the interface between psychology and religion.

This article explores the relation between two quantitative measures of unconscious communication: (a) referential activity, which emerged from the dualcoding model of mental representation; and (b) newness of themes and extent of narrative/imagery, first identified as bearers of derivative material in the communicative approach to psychotherapy. The measures are applied to data from 2 female patients, each of whom was seen separately by 3 psychoanalysts-6 analysts in all—in videotaped consultation interviews. The main findings include significant correlations between referential activity and both newness of themes and extent of narrative/imagery. It was also found that newness of themes and extent of narrative/imagery correlated significantly with each other. Scores on the measures tended to be lowest with the analysts who spoke most often. The limitations of this study, its implications, and the direction of future related work are discussed.

Normal and Pathological Aspects of Self-Descriptions and Their Change Over Long-Term Treatment

Susan Bers PHD , Sidney Blatt PHD , Helen Sayward MS and Rebecca Johnston PHD

Rebecca Johnston

Rebecca Johnston   Is Director of Psychological Services at the deNicola Center for Women's Health in Nashua, NH. Her interests include the process and outcome of psychodynamic psychotherapy and the brief treatment of depression.

Using a new methodology for assessing the subjective experience of the self, the spontaneous self-descriptions of 29 psychiatric inpatients at admission and 40 nonpatients, all females ages 13 to 28, were rated on agency, interpersonal relationships, and cognitive-affective aspects and dimensions. Patients' self-descriptions, compared to nonpatients', were more negative, expressed moderately more depressive feelings and less sense of effectiveness, lacked a sense of both striving and relatedness, used fewer dimensions, and were at a lower developmental cognitive level. Changes in one inpatient's self-descriptions during long-term, intensive treatment were evaluated and compared to independent treatment reviews. They reflected the development of a richer and fuller sense of self including: a more positive and effective self-view; more references to affect, relatedness, and agency; more differentiation; and the use of more dimensions. These changes paralleled independently assessed signs of progress. As behavioral expressions of depression decreased, there was a marked increase in the expression of depression in the patient's self-descriptions, which seemed central to her treatment gains.

Early Object Loss and “Secret Anniversaries of the Heart”

James Hull PHD , Robert Lane PHD and Beverly Gibbons PHD

Beverly Gibbons

Beverly Gibbons   Is Clinical Director of the Adolescent Outdoor Oriented Peer Group Program and Assistant Professor of Psychology at the University of Tennessee at Knoxville.

This article describes a type of anniversary reaction in which the patient has experienced a traumatic early loss and the duration of the therapy relationship functions as an anniversary trigger. Two cases are presented to illustrate how early losses may be repeated in psychotherapy, with the timing of repetition duplicating almost exactly the timing of the original event. Many writers have commented on the ubiquitous power of transference and on the patient's tendency to repeat past traumas; this article suggests that the timing of the original loss also may be brought into the transference.

Psychoanalysis in Education

Attitude and Process

Harriette Kaley PHD

Harriette Kaley

Harriette Kaley   Is Associate Professor at Brooklyn College-City University of New York and is in private practice. Her interests center on educational, supervisory, and clinical processes, on women's issues, and on the history of psychoanalysis. She is currently completing writing projects on dreams and on posttraumatic stress disorder. She is active in the Division of Psychoanalysis.

The purpose and value of introducing psychoanalytic concepts into the professional education of teachers and school psychologists is discussed. Means of approaching the task are described and examples presented, with special emphasis on the importance of conveying to students an analytic attitude which they can adapt for their own work.
